title: Game Theory & Strategic Interaction
simple_html:
Game theory is the study of decisions where your best move depends on what others do — and theirs depends on yours. When a market has only a few firms (an oligopoly), a price cut or new product by one firm provokes reactions from rivals. Game theory gives managers a disciplined way to think through those reactions before acting.

A "game" has three ingredients: players (the decision-makers), strategies (the options each has), and payoffs (what each gets for every combination of choices). The central solution concept is the Nash equilibrium: a set of choices where no player can do better by changing their own move alone, given what everyone else is doing.

The famous example: Prisoner's Dilemma

Two firms each choose "cooperate" (keep prices high) or "defect" (cut prices). Each is individually tempted to cut prices, so both end up cutting — and both earn less than if they had cooperated. The rational individual choice produces a collectively worse outcome. This explains why price wars happen and why cartels are unstable.

Key takeaways

  • Strategic interaction means anticipating rivals' responses, not just optimizing in isolation.
  • A Nash equilibrium is self-enforcing: no one wants to deviate alone.
  • The Prisoner's Dilemma shows why cooperation is hard even when it benefits everyone.
  • Repetition can sustain cooperation — "play nice as long as you do too."
  • A credible threat or commitment is one you'd actually want to carry out.
intermediate_html:
Game theory models strategic interaction: situations where each decision-maker's optimal choice depends on the choices of others. It is the natural language of oligopoly (Lesson 6), where a handful of interdependent firms must each anticipate rivals' reactions. A game is specified by players, strategies, and payoffs, and is analyzed to find an equilibrium — a stable, self-consistent prediction of behavior.

Representing games

Simultaneous-move games use the normal (matrix) form; sequential games use the extensive form (a game tree). The workhorse solution concept is the Nash equilibrium (NE): a strategy profile in which each player's strategy is a best response to the others', so no one can gain by unilaterally deviating.

The Prisoner's Dilemma

Consider two firms choosing High or Low price. Payoffs (Firm A, Firm B):

B: HighB: Low
A: High10, 102, 12
A: Low12, 25, 5

For each firm, Low is a dominant strategy (better regardless of the rival's choice): if B plays High, A gets 12 vs 10; if B plays Low, A gets 5 vs 2. So both play Low and earn (5, 5) — worse than the (10, 10) both could have had. The unique Nash equilibrium is jointly inefficient. This is the canonical model of price wars and cartel breakdown.

Sequential games and credibility

When players move in sequence, solve by backward induction: reason from the last decision back to the first. This yields a subgame-perfect equilibrium, which rules out non-credible threats. Example: an incumbent threatens a price war if an entrant enters. If fighting would actually hurt the incumbent more than accommodating, the threat is empty — backward induction predicts the entrant enters and the incumbent accommodates. To make the threat credible, the incumbent must commit in advance (e.g., build excess capacity), changing its own future payoffs.

Repeated games and cooperation

The grim Prisoner's Dilemma outcome softens with repetition. In an infinitely (or indefinitely) repeated game, cooperation can be a Nash equilibrium sustained by trigger strategies (e.g., "cooperate until you defect, then punish forever") — the logic behind tacit collusion. Cooperation holds when players are patient enough: the discounted value of continued cooperation must exceed the one-time gain from defecting plus the punished future. Axelrod's tournaments (Axelrod, 1984) found simple Tit-for-Tat remarkably effective.

How to apply it

  • Map the game first: who are the players, what can they do, what are the payoffs? Then look for dominant strategies and Nash equilibria.
  • Check credibility: in sequential settings, ignore threats and promises that the player would not actually want to execute. Build commitment to make them real.
  • Exploit repetition: long-term relationships and observable behavior make cooperation (and tacit price coordination) sustainable; one-shot interactions invite defection.
  • Mind first-mover advantages: sometimes committing early (Stackelberg leadership, capacity, reputation) reshapes the game in your favor.

Game theory turns oligopoly from a guessing game into structured reasoning about how rational, interdependent rivals will actually behave.
full_html:
Game theory is the formal analysis of strategic interaction — decisions whose outcomes depend jointly on the choices of multiple rational agents. Founded by von Neumann and Morgenstern's Theory of Games and Economic Behavior (von Neumann & Morgenstern, 1944) and given its central equilibrium concept by John Nash (Nash, 1950), it is the indispensable tool for analyzing oligopoly (Lesson 6), where a few interdependent firms must each forecast rivals' reactions to price, output, entry, and investment decisions.

Elements and representations

A game comprises players, their available strategies, and payoffs over every strategy combination, plus the information and timing structure. Static (simultaneous-move) games are written in normal form (a payoff matrix); dynamic (sequential) games use the extensive form (a game tree specifying order of moves and information sets). Games may be of complete or incomplete information (whether payoffs are common knowledge), and of perfect or imperfect information (whether prior moves are observed).

Solution concepts

Dominance. A strategy is strictly dominant if it yields a higher payoff than any alternative regardless of others' choices; iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies can sometimes solve a game outright. Nash equilibrium (NE) generalizes this: a profile of strategies, one per player, such that each is a best response to the others, so no player can profitably deviate unilaterally. Nash (1950) proved that every finite game has at least one equilibrium, possibly in mixed strategies — randomizations over pure strategies. In a mixed-strategy equilibrium each player randomizes so as to make rivals indifferent among their own options (e.g., the Matching Pennies and entry-deterrence games have only mixed equilibria).

Subgame-perfect equilibrium (SPE). For sequential games, Selten (1965) refined NE to require optimal play in every subgame, computed by backward induction. SPE eliminates non-credible threats — threats a player would not rationally execute when the moment arrives.

Bayesian and Perfect Bayesian equilibrium. Harsanyi (1967–68) extended analysis to incomplete information by modeling uncertainty as a probability distribution over player "types," yielding Bayesian Nash equilibrium; combined with sequential rationality and belief updating, this gives Perfect Bayesian equilibrium, the framework for signaling and reputation games.

The Prisoner's Dilemma and its lessons

The Prisoner's Dilemma is the most consequential game in social science. Two firms choose High or Low price; payoffs (A, B):

B: HighB: Low
A: High10, 102, 12
A: Low12, 25, 5

Low strictly dominates High for both players, so the unique NE is (Low, Low) with payoffs (5, 5) — Pareto-inferior to (High, High) = (10, 10). The dilemma formalizes the tension between individual and collective rationality and explains price wars, the instability of cartels, free-riding on public goods (Lesson 9), and arms races. The structural cause is that the cooperative outcome is not a Nash equilibrium of the one-shot game.

Quantity competition: the Cournot model as a game

Oligopoly's classic models are games in disguise. In Cournot competition (Cournot, 1838), firms choose quantities simultaneously. With demand P = a − bQ and identical marginal cost c, each firm's reaction function is its best-response output given the rival's. With two firms the symmetric Nash equilibrium is q* = (a − c) / 3b each, total Q = 2(a − c)/3b — output between monopoly and perfect competition. Numeric example: with a = 100, b = 1, c = 10, each firm produces q* = 90/3 = 30, total Q = 60, price P = 40; compare monopoly (Q = 45, P = 55) and competition (Q = 90, P = 10). The Bertrand model (price competition) instead drives price to marginal cost with homogeneous goods — the "Bertrand paradox." The Stackelberg model converts the game to sequential moves, giving the leader a first-mover advantage solved by backward induction.

Credible commitment and entry deterrence

Schelling's insight (Schelling, 1960) is that strategic advantage often comes from limiting one's own options. A threat or promise matters only if it is credible — incentive-compatible to carry out. An incumbent's threat to fight entry with a price war is non-credible if accommodation is more profitable; backward induction then predicts entry. But the incumbent can make the threat credible through commitment: irreversibly building excess capacity, signing most-favored-customer clauses, or investing in a reputation for toughness (Dixit, 1980; Milgrom & Roberts, 1982 on predation and reputation). Commitment works precisely because it alters the committer's own future payoffs, changing the subgame-perfect outcome.

Repeated games and sustaining cooperation

Repetition transforms the dilemma. In an infinitely (or indefinitely) repeated Prisoner's Dilemma, the Folk Theorem shows that cooperation can be sustained as a subgame-perfect equilibrium via trigger strategies (e.g., grim trigger: cooperate until any defection, then revert to the one-shot NE forever) — provided players are sufficiently patient. Cooperation holds when the per-period gain from cheating is outweighed by the discounted loss of future cooperation: cooperation is sustainable when the discount factor satisfies δ ≥ (T − C) / (T − N), where T is the temptation payoff, C the cooperative payoff, and N the Nash punishment payoff. This is the formal logic of tacit collusion among oligopolists and the reason long-lived, observable relationships support coordination that one-shot encounters destroy. Axelrod's (1984) computer tournaments famously found the simple, forgiving, retaliatory Tit-for-Tat strategy to be robustly successful.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating threats and promises as automatically binding. Only credible (subgame-perfect) commitments shape rational behavior; cheap talk does not.
  • Assuming a unique equilibrium. Many games (coordination games, repeated games) have multiple equilibria; selecting among them (focal points, risk-dominance) is a separate problem.
  • Ignoring mixed strategies. Some games have no pure-strategy NE; insisting on deterministic play misses the equilibrium entirely.
  • Over-applying the one-shot Prisoner's Dilemma. Most business relationships are repeated; the cooperative outcome may be perfectly rational.
  • Confusing Nash with Pareto-optimal. Equilibrium is about stability, not efficiency — the two often diverge.

Game theory reframes oligopoly and competitive strategy as structured reasoning about interdependent rational agents. It connects directly to Market Structures (Lesson 6) through the Cournot, Bertrand, and Stackelberg models, to Pricing Practices (Lesson 7) when rivals react to pricing menus, and to Market Failure (Lesson 9) through public-goods free-riding and signaling under asymmetric information.
